original_hypothesis.title: WhitelistDeployer is permissionless: any attacker can deploy arbitrary whitelist contracts (spoofing/private-sale confusion)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer.deployWhitelist() is external and permissionless. An attacker can deploy look-alike whitelist contracts with misleading parameters/events (e.g., same lotOwner) and potentially trick integrators/off-chain services that rely on emitted WhitelistCreated events from the deployer address rather than the actual listing.whitelist stored in Marketplace.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While Marketplace uses the configured whitelistDeployer address and stores the resulting whitelist address per listing, many production systems index factory events to infer which whitelists are 'official'. Permissionless factory calls can create event noise and spoofed 'official' whitelists, leading to practical integration and access-control failures.
deployment_assumptions: Off-chain indexers/UI/backend treat any WhitelistCreated event from the canonical SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer as authoritative for private lots (instead of reading Marketplace.listings[...].whitelist)
Users rely on those off-chain lists to decide eligibility or to join the whitelist
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No on-chain registry check exists in code; risk depends on off-chain consumption of SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer events.

proof_of_signal.pattern: external deployWhitelist() has no access control; emits WhitelistCreated for any caller

preconditions: At least one downstream component (UI/indexer/backoffice) trusts factory events without cross-checking listing.whitelist

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Event-based spoofing can mislead private-sale eligibility tooling
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No caller restriction; no linkage to a specific marketplace/listing ID in event

analysis_questions: Do official frontends/indexers derive whitelist addresses from events rather than listing storage?
Is the WhitelistCreated event in Marketplace used as the only authoritative signal instead (safer)?
Are there any on-chain checks that the whitelist is tied to a listing?
validation_requirements: Review subgraph/indexer logic (if any) for whitelist discovery
Attempt spoof deployment and verify whether any system mistakenly recognizes it

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys multiple whitelists via the canonical deployer, setting lotOwner to a known seller/admin to appear legitimate, then shares the whitelist address to victims/integrators; victims may whitelist themselves on the wrong contract and fail eligibility checks later, or private-sale gating may be misapplied by an indexer.
prerequisites: No additional signature/attestation scheme tying whitelist to listing

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect: failed purchases, user funds wasted on gas, private-sale access confusion
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Private sale participants and sellers relying on whitelist tooling
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Operational; fix indexers/UI; not easily remediated on-chain

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing integration tests validating whitelist discovery source of truth

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare event-derived whitelist lists vs Marketplace storage

validation_result.mechanism: permissionless factory with no access control
validation_result.impact_summary: SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer.deployWhitelist() has no access control, allowing anyone to deploy whitelist contracts and emit WhitelistCreated events from the canonical deployer address, potentially misleading off-chain systems.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis correctly identifies that SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer.deployWhitelist() at lines 19-40 is completely permissionless. The function has no modifier, no require on msg.sender, and no restriction on parameters. Anyone can call it with arbitrary _maxWhitelist and _lotOwner values, and a real SecondSwap_Whitelist contract will be deployed and a WhitelistCreated event emitted from the canonical deployer address.

On-chain, the impact is limited because the Marketplace correctly stores the whitelist address in listings[vestingPlan][listingId].whitelist and uses only that stored address for purchase validation via IWhitelist(listing.whitelist).validateAddress(msg.sender). However, the deployer emits WhitelistCreated(whitelistContract, _lotOwner) which is indistinguishable from legitimate marketplace-triggered deployments when observed purely from the deployer's event log.

This constitutes a missing access control safeguard. The function is only intended to be called by the Marketplace contract (as evidenced by SecondSwap_Marketplace.sol line ~246 where it calls the deployer via the settings contract), but no enforcement exists. The deployer should restrict callers to authorized contracts.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer.sol lines 19-40 confirm the function is external with zero access control. SecondSwap_Marketplace.sol line ~246 shows only the Marketplace is the intended caller. The Marketplace also emits its own WhitelistCreated event (with vestingPlan and listingId) which is more specific, but the deployer's event exists independently and provides a spoofing vector.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No access control: any address can call this function, deploying a whitelist contract and emitting a WhitelistCreated event from the canonical deployer address. The function should be restricted to authorized callers (e.g., the Marketplace contract).

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls deployWhitelist(_maxWhitelist, victimSellerAddress) on the canonical SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer
2. No access control check exists - function proceeds unconditionally
3. New SecondSwap_Whitelist contract is deployed with attacker-chosen parameters
4. WhitelistCreated event is emitted from the canonical deployer address with the spoofed _lotOwner
5. Off-chain indexers monitoring the canonical deployer see an authoritative-looking event
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The function at SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer.sol:30-34 has no require() statements checking msg.sender. It unconditionally deploys a new SecondSwap_Whitelist and emits WhitelistCreated. Compare with the intended usage in SecondSwap_Marketplace.sol:~246 where listVesting() calls whitelistDeployer.deployWhitelist() - this is the only legitimate caller, but no enforcement exists.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Off-chain indexers/UI/backend treat any WhitelistCreated event from the canonical SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer as authoritative for private lots
Users rely on those off-chain lists to decide eligibility or to join the whitelist
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts (DeployWhitelistDeployer.ts and DeploySecondSwap.s.sol) deploy SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer with no access control setup. The deployer has no mechanism to restrict callers - it is a stateless factory with no admin or authorized caller configuration. No indexer/subgraph configuration is provided in the deployment scripts.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is straightforward and requires zero capital: (1) Attacker identifies the canonical SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer address from deployment records or MarketplaceSetting.whitelistDeployer. (2) Attacker calls deployWhitelist(100, knownSellerAddress) multiple times with known seller addresses. (3) Each call emits WhitelistCreated from the canonical address with a legitimate-looking _lotOwner. (4) Any off-chain system indexing these events without cross-referencing Marketplace.listings[vestingPlan][listingId].whitelist may treat these as legitimate private sale whitelists. (5) Users directed to these spoofed whitelists would whitelist themselves on wrong contracts and fail eligibility checks on real listings, or waste gas.
validation_result.impact_assessment: On-chain: minimal direct impact - the Marketplace correctly stores and validates whitelist addresses from listings storage, not from events. Off-chain: moderate impact - if indexers/UI use deployer events as the source of truth for whitelist discovery (rather than Marketplace listing storage), attackers can create confusion about which whitelists are legitimate. Users may whitelist on wrong contracts, failing eligibility for actual private sales. Gas is wasted. The severity is MEDIUM because the on-chain protocol remains secure, but the missing access control is a real code defect that enables integration-level attacks.
recommended_mitigations: Add an authorized caller mapping or a single authorized address (the Marketplace) to SecondSwap_WhitelistDeployer, restricting who can call deployWhitelist()
Alternative: remove the WhitelistCreated event from the deployer entirely, since the Marketplace already emits its own more specific WhitelistCreated event with vestingPlan and listingId

Ensure off-chain indexers derive whitelist addresses exclusively from Marketplace.listings storage or the Marketplace's WhitelistCreated event (which includes vestingPlan and listingId), not from the deployer's event
Consider making the deployer address configurable with an owner/admin who can set the authorized marketplace caller
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy the full SecondSwap system using the provided deployment scripts. 2. Note the canonical WhitelistDeployer address from MarketplaceSetting.whitelistDeployer(). 3. From an arbitrary EOA (not the marketplace), call deployWhitelist(10, sellerAddress) on the canonical deployer. 4. Verify the WhitelistCreated event is emitted successfully. 5. Verify the deployed whitelist is a real SecondSwap_Whitelist contract. 6. Demonstrate that an indexer monitoring WhitelistCreated events from the deployer cannot distinguish attacker-created whitelists from marketplace-created ones without cross-referencing listing storage.
uncertainties: Whether any production off-chain system (subgraph, indexer, UI) actually uses the deployer's WhitelistCreated event as a source of truth - no indexer code was provided
Whether the protocol team considers permissionless whitelist deployment an intentional design choice rather than a missing safeguard
The practical likelihood of users being misled depends on the specific off-chain architecture which is not in scope
additional_testing_needed: Review any subgraph/indexer code for whitelist discovery logic
Verify that frontend/UI reads whitelist addresses from Marketplace.listings rather than deployer events
Test whether emitting fake WhitelistCreated events causes any observable misbehavior in the production stack
